title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who learned a traditional craft. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### David, Blacksmithing
'The heat of the forge is something you never forget,' David says. [47]I was quite startled by how much the physical environment affected my ability to concentrate initially.[/47] [53]I had to learn that you can't force the metal; you have to work with its temperature and rhythm.[/53] [51]The most challenging part was the sheer repetition required to master even a simple nail.[/51] [56]If I hadn't inherited my grandfather's old tools, I probably would never have considered such an expensive hobby.[/56] [49]I found that the focus required for blacksmithing provided a perfect mental break from my high-stress job in finance.[/49] [52]I’d initially worried that it was a dying art with no practical use today, but I’ve found a huge market for hand-forged gates.
texts.B:
### Elena, Silk Weaving
Elena traveled to a small village to learn traditional weaving. 'It’s like a complex mathematical puzzle,' she explains. [50]I found that my background in computer programming actually helped me understand the logic of the loom patterns.[/50] [48]I was surprised by how much my posture improved after hours of sitting at the frame.[/48] [55]It took months of practice before I could produce a single meter of fabric without a visible mistake.[/55] [53]The experience taught me that real quality cannot be rushed, a lesson that has spilled over into my personal life.[/53] [47]Initially, the noise of the looms was incredibly distracting, but eventually, it became a soothing background hum.[/47] It’s a hypnotic process.
texts.C:
### Marcus, Stone Carving
Marcus took up stone carving after a trip to Italy. 'It’s a very unforgiving medium,' he notes. [51]The biggest difficulty was the psychological pressure of knowing that one wrong strike could ruin weeks of work.[/51] [54]I was amazed by how much my appreciation for architecture changed after I understood the effort behind a single pillar.[/54] [52]I had been concerned that it would be too solitary, but the carving studio turned out to be a vibrant social hub.[/52] [48]I noticed that my grip strength increased significantly, which was a nice bonus I hadn't thought about.[/48] [49]The hobby has given me a sense of permanence in a world that often feels very temporary.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have known where to start if I hadn't found a local mentor willing to take on a novice.
texts.D:
### Sarah, Bookbinding
Sarah learned to bind books using 18th-century techniques. 'I love the smell of leather and glue,' she says. [50]I realized that the precision I used in my previous career as a laboratory technician was perfectly suited to this craft.[/50] [55]It was incredibly frustrating at first, as I spent hours on tasks that only took my teacher minutes.[/55] [54]I was genuinely shocked by how much I enjoyed the historical research required for each project.[/54] [52]I’d initially feared that it would be an elitist or inaccessible world, but the other binders were very down-to-earth.[/52] [49]The craft has taught me to appreciate the physical object in an era of digital books.[/49] [51]The most difficult part was finding the authentic materials required for traditional methods.

questions.question: mentions an initial struggle with the sensory conditions of their workplace?
questions.explanation: David (A) was startled by the heat affecting his concentration, and Elena (B) found the noise distracting.

questions.question: mentions a specific physical improvement they noticed in themselves?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) noticed improved posture, and Marcus (C) noticed increased grip strength.

questions.question: explains how the craft provides a sense of balance or a new perspective on the modern world?
questions.explanation: David (A) found a break from finance, and Sarah (D) appreciates physical objects in a digital era.

questions.question: points out that their previous professional experience was relevant to the craft?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) used programming logic, and Sarah (D) used lab technician precision.

questions.question: describes a particular challenge related to the technique or materials?
questions.explanation: David (A) mentions repetition, Marcus (C) mentions the pressure of mistakes, and Sarah (D) mentions finding materials.

questions.question: mentions that their initial assumptions about the social or practical aspect were wrong?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) found the studio social, and Sarah (D) found binders down-to-earth.

questions.question: reflects on a broader life lesson they learned through the craft?
questions.explanation: David (A) learned to work with the metal's rhythm, and Elena (B) learned that quality cannot be rushed.

questions.question: mentions gaining a new appreciation for things they previously took for granted?
questions.explanation: Marcus (C) changed his view on architecture, and Sarah (D) enjoyed the historical research.

questions.question: describes feeling frustration due to the slow rate of progress at the beginning?
questions.explanation: Elena (B) took months to produce fabric, and Sarah (D) was frustrated by how slow she was compared to her teacher.

questions.question: acknowledges that their entry into the craft was made possible by an external factor?
questions.explanation: David (A) inherited tools, and Marcus (C) found a mentor.
